coredata.dc:title: Accurate ethanol determination in Chinese Baijiu based on red-emitted carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and a simple pH correction
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Xin-He
Li, Yi
Li, Hong-Wei
Wu, Yuqing
coredata.dc:description: The quality control of Baijiu is necessary including ethanol content. In this work, red-emitted carbon quantum dots based on p-phenylenediamine (PPD-CQDs) were fabricated for accurate determination of ethanol in Baijiu. The good linear response of PPD-CQDs to ethanol–water solution (E-Ws; 20–80 vol%) offered the potential for ethanol determination in practice. However, further application of this procedure to Baijiu resulted in a certain deviation. After excluding interference from most of the ingredients involved, a simple pH correction on Baijiu made application of the process practicable. The red-emission of PPD-CQDs supported analysis of Baijiu with different flavors and ethanol monitoring. Therefore, a quick-response fluorescence method was established for Baijiu ethanol determination; this method can be applied to the many varieties of Chinese Baijiu available and also has potential for use in other alcoholic beverages.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red emission CQDs
Ethanol determination
Chinese Baijiu
pH correction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence determination of ethanol in E-Ws
Fluorescence determination of ethanol in Baijiu samples
The fluorescence responses of CQDs to ethanol
Fluorescence determination of ethanol content in Baijiu by PPD-CQDs
Emission deviation between scE-Ws and Baijiu
Crucial factors leading to the emission gap of PPD-CQDs between Baijiu and scE-Ws
Correction of the emission deviation between scE-Ws and Baijiu through pH adjustment
Determination of ethanol contents in Baijiu.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CQDs. (A-C) TEM images and particle size distribution histogram (insert), (D-F) UV–vis absorption spectra, and (G-I) fluorescence spectra of MPD-CQDs, OPD-CQDs and PPD-CQDs in water, respectively.
(A) FT-IR spectra of MPD-CQDs, OPD-CQDs, PPD-CQDs, and XPS spectra of (B) MPD-CQDs, (C) OPD-CQDs and (D) PPD-CQDs.

Fluorescence spectra of the (A) MPD-CQDs, (B) OPD-CQDs, and (C) PPD-CQDs in response to different concentrations of ethanol. (D) Relationships between the ratio of fluorescence intensity (F/F 0) and the ethanol concentrations for MPD-CQDs, OPD-CQDs, and PPD-CQDs.

Fluorescence spectra of the (A) PPD-CQDs upon dilution and concentration of 53% Baijiu. (B) The fluorescence intensity ratios (F/F 0) of PPD-CQDs responding to different concentrations of ethanol in E-Ws (0–100%; red) and in real Baijiu (yellow, green and blue). (C-F) Fluorescence spectra of PPD-CQDs responding to NaOH concentrations in four different kinds of Light aroma Baijiu. (G) Diagrams of the changes in PPD-CQDs fluorescence intensity with NaOH concentration in four representative Light aroma Baijiu samples. (H) A linear relationship between the F/F 0 of PPD-CQDs and the ethanol concentration in E-Ws, which will be used as a working plot to evaluate the ethanol contents in Baijiu.
Processes for CQDs fabrication and the application of red-emitted PPD-CQDs to the ethanol determination of Baijiu.
The ethanol content (vol%) in different degrees of Baijiu samples collected from the market using PPD-CQDs as fluorescent probes.

head.ce:title: Accurate ethanol determination in Chinese Baijiu based on red-emitted carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and a simple pH correction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, No. 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, No. 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China Light Industry Enterprises Investment and Development Association, 22B. Fuwai Avenue, Xicheng District, Beijing 100833, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China Light Industry Enterprises Investment and Development Association

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: China Light Industry Enterprises Investment and Development Association, 22B. Fuwai Avenue, Xicheng District, Beijing 100833, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Theoretical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, No. 2 Liutiao Road, Changchun 130023, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Theoretical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, No. 2 Liutiao Road, Changchun 130023, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Theoretical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, No. 2 Liutiao Road, Changchun 130023, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The quality control of Baijiu is necessary including ethanol content. In this work, red-emitted carbon quantum dots based on p-phenylenediamine (PPD-CQDs) were fabricated for accurate determination of ethanol in Baijiu. The good linear response of PPD-CQDs to ethanol–water solution (E-Ws; 20–80 vol%) offered the potential for ethanol determination in practice. However, further application of this procedure to Baijiu resulted in a certain deviation. After excluding interference from most of the ingredients involved, a simple pH correction on Baijiu made application of the process practicable. The red-emission of PPD-CQDs supported analysis of Baijiu with different flavors and ethanol monitoring. Therefore, a quick-response fluorescence method was established for Baijiu ethanol determination; this method can be applied to the many varieties of Chinese Baijiu available and also has potential for use in other alcoholic beverages.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Chinese liquor, known as Baijiu in China, is a famous distilled spirit with a production history of over 2000 years (Liu & Sun, 2018). In China, Baijiu production is one of the most booming industries, with an annual yield of approximately 5 million liters. It is one of the most widely consumed beverages in China and has significant commercial value and social importance. Baijiu is essentially composed of ethanol and water, as well as ingredients that provide different flavors (Zheng et al., 2018). Alcohol content, which is the volume percentage of ethanol, is not only the legal basis for local, import and export taxes but also the basis for industry standards and quality control. In particular, in a qualified Baijiu, the indicated ethanol content must be very accurate; a high alcohol content can be harmful to the human body, while a low alcohol content can decrease the quality of Baijiu, further affect the commercial reputation of manufacturers. Therefore, it is very important to detect the ethanol content in Baijiu. Moreover, along with the rapid increase in alcoholic beverage consumption worldwide, food safety based on ethanol quantification is becoming important. Great efforts should be made to develop an effective method to ensure the quality of Baijiu, as well as to avoid counterfeits.

The developed techniques for alcohol determination in distilled spirit include distillation (Cloninger, 2018), alcohol densitometry (Lachenmeier et al., 2003) and spectrophotometric methods (Fletcher & van Staden, 2003), while the time-consuming process and large sample volume limit their applications for online detection. In addition, other spectral analyses based on sophisticated instruments, such as Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Gallignani et al., 1994), near-infrared spectrophotometry (NIR) (Debebe et al., 2017), and Raman spectroscopy (Boyaci et al., 2012), have also been developed. These methods are complicated, costly, and laborious. Compared with the above methods, the fluorescence method is simple and rapid and can be used for online detection. Early fluorescence-based alcohol detection utilized fluorescent dyes such as Nile blue (Petrova et al., 2007), porphyrins (Bozkurt et al., 2010), and Schiff bases (Kumar et al., 2015). Ethanol detection in alcoholic beverages using a nanofibrous polymer containing fluorescent molecules was developed with more responsiveness but less accuracy (Akamatsu et al., 2015). Apart from fluorescent dyes, luminescent materials such as CdS quantum dots (QDs) were later applied to construct a sensitive gas sensor for alcohol detection (Jiao et al., 2013). However, most of the above studies in this field are only stopped at the detection of alcohol, and much more effort should be devoted to improving the fluorescence method for the accurate determination of ethanol content in Baijiu.

Since their first discovery in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been a hot topic of investigation due to their excellent stability, low cost, and good biocompatibility. Beyond the extensive use of CQDs in versatile fields such as solar cells, capacitors, detection of small molecules, and bioimages (Xiao et al., 2021; Meng et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2022), researchers have tried to synthesize CQDs with different emissions using a variety of raw materials and methods. However, under the excitation of ultraviolet light, the emission wavelengths of CQDs are mostly located in the blue or green region, which easily overlap with the strong emission of some kinds of ingredients in alcoholic beverages (Jia et al., 2022), consequently leading to inaccurate results. Therefore, red-emitted CQDs are promising for accurate ethanol determination, which can eliminate the interference of intrinsic fluorescence in Baijiu.

In this work, the red-emitted carbon quantum dots based on p-phenylenediamine (PPD-CQDs) were fabricated, which showed a good linear response to ethanol contents in E-Ws. Such a process was supposed to extend to ethanol determination in practice; however, further application to Chinese Baijiu resulted in a certain deviation compared with the same concentration of E-W solution (scE-Ws). After excluding the interference of most ingredients involved, a simple pH correction of Baijiu led to accurate ethanol determination in actual samples. Therefore, a fast-response fluorescence method was established for ethanol determination in Chinese Baijiu (Scheme 1 ). The red emission of PPD-CQDs efficiently avoided the interference of aromatic ingredients of Baijiu on ethanol detection, making PPD-CQDs applicable widely to various flavor types of Baijiu. Several advantages can be summarized for the developed method for ethanol determination in Baijiu: (1) it is direct and easy to operate via only a pH correction; (2) quick response within seconds, allowing rapid quality control of Baijiu; and (3) good stability, as the red emission of PPD-CQDs can be kept for months in water. Therefore, a fast-response fluorescence method was established for ethanol determination in Chinese Baijiu, which not only can be applied to versatile Chinese Baijiu but also has potential for use in other alcoholic beverages.

Meta-phenylenediamine (MPD), ortho-phenylenediamine (OPD), para-phenylenediamine (PPD), citric acid, malic acid, lactic acid, acetal, methanol, ethyl acetate, 1-propanol, lactic acid, n-butanol, isobutanol, and isopentyl alcohol were purchased from Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. of Aladdin (Shanghai, China), and their purities were greater than 99%. Acetic acid, n-octanoic acid, propionic acid, caproic acid, and heptanoic acid were purchased from Macklin BiochemicalTechnologyCo., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Branded Baijiu was obtained from a local supermarket (Changchun, China), while the samples from online distillation were supplied by the factory of Huanghuai Institution (Zhumadian, China). Ethanol (HPLC grade), used for E-Ws solution and Baijiu condense, was purchased from Yuhe New Materials (Yucheng, China). Ultrapure water (ρ = 18.2 MΩ·cm, 25 °C), produced by a purification system of Changchun Ultra-Pure Technology Co., Ltd. (Changchun, China), was used throughout the experiments for dilution.

Fluorescence (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan) and UV–vis absorption (UV-3600, PerkinElmer Inc., Massachusetts, USA) spectrophotometers were used to record the corresponding spectra. JEM-2200FS equipment (Jeol Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) was used to measure the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images with a voltageof 200 kV for acceleration. A Thermo Fischer ESCALAB 250Xi was used to measure the X-ray photoelectron spectrum using an Al K-AlphaX-ray source (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, USA). An FLS920 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., Livingston, UK) was used to record the time-resolved fluorescence spectra.

A hydrothermal method (Liu et al., 2020) was used to synthesize the CQDs after slight optimization. In summary, 0.02 g MPD, OPD, and PPD were dissolved in 10.0 mL distilled water. The solution was mixed with 10 mL citric acid (1 M), which was moved to a steel autoclave with Teflon lined stainless steel. After heating at 180 ℃ for 10 h, a cylinder membrane (0.22 μm) was used to exclude the large CQDs of the production. Next, dialysis (500 Da dialysis membrane) was performed on the solution for 10 h by using large amounts of distilled water. Finally, the CQDs solution was obtained and stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination of ethanol in E-Ws
ce:section.ce:para: First, different volumes of ethanol (99.7%) were mixed with ultrapure water to prepare various concentrations of E-Ws in volume percentages between 0 and 100 vol%, in a total of 1 mL. Then, CQDs (10 μL) were added into 1.0 mL E-Ws containing 0–100% (vol%) ethanol in 10% (vol%) increments and each concentration of ethanol was tested three times in parallel. Fluorescence spectra were collected under excitation at 500 nm and the intensity of the characteristic emission peaks was used for construction of the working plot and further quantitation of Baijiu.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination of ethanol in Baijiu samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of real Baijiu, 28 samples were chosen with same or different aromas. In particular, among each aroma the Baijiu samples with same or different ethanol degree, were selected from different origins. In details, 9 Light-, 6 Strong-, 6 Sauce-, and 7 other-aroma Baijiu were tested to assay the biological replicates of the developed method. For that, PPD-CQDs (10 μL) was mixed with each Baijiu in centrifugal tube, where the pH was adjusted by adding 10 μL of 1.0 M NaOH, and the final volume was fixed at 1 mL. For each Baijiu, five samples were prepared in parallel to get more confident results. The fluorescence spectra were then measured for the solutions after thorough mixing (λex = 500 nm). Finally, the ethanol content of Baijiu was evaluated from the working plot.

The morphology and size of the synthesized CQDs were measured by TEM images (Fig. 1 ), which are shown separately in uniform spheres. After statistical analysis, mean sizes of 1.64 nm for MPD-CQDs, 1.65 nm for OPD-CQDs, and 2.04 nm for PPD-CQDs were achieved. The close particle sizes between them indicate that the particle sizes are not the predominant factor determining the emission colors (Wei et al., 2020), at least in the present work. Their optical properties were then verified by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. The absorption peaks are revealed between 200 and 300 nm for all three CQDs, corresponding to the π–π* transition of CC and CN bonds (Wang et al., 2017). In addition, there is a wide absorption between 400 and 600 nm for PPD- and OPD-CQDs, corresponding to the n–π* transition of CN or CO (Bai et al., 2022).

In addition, the emission spectra of MPD-CQDs andOPD-CQDs show faint excitation-dependent emission between 510 and 540 nm when using excitation wavelengths between 400 and 460 nm. However, PPD-CQDs exhibit constant red emission with a maximum at 610 nm under moving excitation between 400 and 520 nm, being not excitation-dependent (Fig. 1). As reported, the excited line-dependent fluorescence was usually ascribed to the distinguished photon absorption mechanisms of the CQDs originating from the different emissive sites on CQDs (Hu et al., 2015), while the excited line-independent luminescence for PPD-CQDs is due to the emission from the uniform defects on the surface (Dong et al., 2013). Therefore, the constant red emission of PPD-CQDs might essentially originate from the constant chemical groups at the surface, being intrinsically different from the green emission of MPD-CQDs and yellow emission of OPD-CQDs.

FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were carried out (Fig. 2 ) to explore the differences in surface chemistry between MPD-, OPD- and PPD-CQDs. First, several new peaks characteristic of the stretching models of NH (∼3129 cm−1), CN (1402 cm−1) and COO– (1386 cm−1) (Huo et al., 2021; Mondal et al., 2018; Xia et al., 2019) appeared for each CQDs compared to the respective material (Fig. 2A). Their appearances suggest that intermolecular cyclization and/or condensation reactions of the raw materials (Xia et al., 2019) occur during the formation of CQDs, indicating that aromatic structures and/or O/N-containing groups are conjugated at the surface of CQDs. Of note, the bands assigned for OH (3328 cm−1), CN (1635 cm−1), CC (1601 cm−1), and CN (1515 cm−1) (Bai et al., 2022) are maintained in PPD-CQDs, but they almost disappear in OPD-CQDs and MPD-CQDs. Most peaks in PPD-CQDs indicate that its surface chemical groups are more abundant. In particular, the content of CN (1515 cm−1) in PPD-CQDs is obviously higher than that in OPD-CQDs and MPD-CQDs, indicating that the amino content may be crucial to the red emission of PPD-CQDs.

In addition, the XPS spectra illustrate that the three CQDs all have C, N and O, but their ratios are different. As illustrated (Fig. 2B-D), all three CQDs have close amounts of carbon (∼66–70%) but large deviations in the N and O contents, where the nitrogen content in PPD-CQDs is the highest among them. The C1s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. S1 A, D, G) of OPD-CQDs and PPD-CQDs can be split into four peaks at 289.0, 286.0, 285.2, 284.5 eV, which are assigned to CN, CO, CN, or CC/CC, respectively (Huo et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019; Mondal et al., 2018). However, the deconvolution of the C1s spectrum for MPD-CQDs shows only three peaks at 286.0, 285.2 and 284.5 eV, which are assigned to the CO, CN, or CC/CC bond, respectively. The integrated area of each peak reveals its relative content in materials. Of note, a high content of CN in PPD-CQDs is obvious, indicating that the red emission of PPD-CQDs is directly related to the amino N, as indicated by the FT-IR spectrum above. The O1s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. S1 B, E, H) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 533.2, 532.7 and 532.2 eV, originating from OH, COC and CO bonds (Huo et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2019; Xia et al., 2019). In addition, the N1s spectrum (Fig. S1 C, F, I) is split into three parts, assigned to pyrrolic N (∼400.5 eV), amino N (∼399.2 eV) and pyridinic N (∼398.7 eV) (Bai et al., 2022; Xia et al., 2019; Yue et al., 2019). The best parameter contributing to the red emission is the increased amount of amino N at 399.2 eV. In the present study, we suppose that surface states predominated by amino nitrogen are conducive to the red emission of PPD-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence responses of CQDs to ethanol
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence responses of CQDs toward ethanol at different concentrations are measured. As illustrated, the emission intensity of CQDs increases with increasing ethanol concentration (Fig. 3 A-C). Meanwhile, the maximum enhancements of these three CQDs in ethanol are different, i.e., 1.85-fold for MPD-CQDs, 9.19-fold for OPD-CQDs, and 12.97-fold for PPD-CQDs.

The intensities of the major peak were then used to establish the relationship between the CQDs emission and ethanol concentration and thus were used to determine the working ranges. Fig. 3D shows that the spectra of MPD-CQDs increases weakly with increasing ethanol concentration, where a strained linear response is shown over the range of 10–40 vol% (R2 = 0.9990). In parallel, the emission intensity of OPD-CQDs versus ethanol concentrations ranging from 0 to 100% was also explored, where a linear response in a wider range of 20–60 vol% (R2 = 0.9960) was established for ethanol. For the PPD-CQDs, however, the fluorescence increases sharply along with ethanol condensation in the range of 0–100 vol%. A glance at the trend found a turn at the point of 20 vol%. Therefore, a curve fitting analysis was first performed in the range of 20–100 vol%. However, the relatively poor regression line (R2 = 0.9889) pushed us to further improve it. Considering that the ethanol degree of Chinese Baijiu cannot exceed 80 vol%, to gain more accurate determination in practice, we focused on a narrower range of 20–80 vol%. A curve fitting on the plot in this region supplied a better linear response (R2 = 0.9978) of F/F 0 = 0.13⋅Cethanol-0.36, which established a reliable working plot for ethanol determination in the range of 20–80 vol%. Because of the outstanding advantages of red emission at 610 nm, linear response to the wide ethanol concentration, and the steeper response slope toward ethanol over the other two CQDs, PPD-CQDs will be selected to determine the ethanol content in practice.

UV–vis absorption (Fig. 1D–F), fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3) and fluorescence lifetime (Fig. S2) were combined to reveal the response mechanism of the three CQDs to ethanol. As shown in Fig. 1D–F, moving from pure water to ethanol induces a small redshift from 288 nm to 292 nm for MPD-CQDs, a large blueshift from 451 nm to 423 nm for OPD-CQDs, and a small blueshift from 506 nm to 500 nm for PPD-CQDs, suggesting that ethanol changes the n-π* or π-π* transition of CQDs. In addition, in Fig. 3, the emission peaks of CQDs are blue shifted with increasing ethanol concentration, which may be due to the polarity decrease of the solvent. In addition, the photoluminescence decay curves of MPD-CQDs, OPD-CQDs and PPD-CQDs were recorded in H2O and ethanol. As revealed in Fig. S2, the fluorescence lifetimes (τ) change slightly from 6.97 ns to 6.40 ns for MPD-CQDs but largely from 1.99 ns to 4.21 ns for OPD-CQDs and from 2.02 ns to 5.91 ns for PPD-CQDs. Such data indicate that the fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs are not determined by the solvent polarity, while hydrogen bonds between the surface –NH2 and ethanol may play an essential key role in solvation (Mu et al., 2022). Therefore, the fluorescence response of these CQDs to ethanol could be explained by the fact that along with the increases in ethanol, the hydrogen bonding between CQDs and ethanol becomes predominant. CQDs interact with solvents via hydrogen bonds, which can gradually weaken because water has the highest polarity. This may lead to redistribution of the electron cloud, which may influence the conjugation degree of n-π* or π-π*, and raise the related energy level. Finally, the emission intensity of CQDs gradually increases with increasing ethanol concentration. In addition, according to the different enhancing efficiencies of the three CQDs in ethanol, it is speculated that a higher amino content allows more hydrogen bonds to be easily formed, which affect the conjugation degree of n-π* or π-π*, leading to more extensive enhancement.

Considering the advantages of the green synthesis process, red emission, and highly sensitive and attractive linear response to ethanol of PPD-CQDs, a new fluorescence probe was developed to detect ethanol in the E-Ws, which lays the foundation to further detect the ethanol degree in real liquor, such as Chinese Baijiu.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination of ethanol content in Baijiu by PPD-CQDs
Emission deviation between scE-Ws and Baijiu
ce:section.ce:para: First, the fluorescence intensity of a Light aroma Baijiu with 53 vol% ethanol, along with solutions diluted with water or concentrated with ethanol, was measured as a representative response to PPD-CQDs (Fig. 4 A). Later, the fluorescence intensity ratios (F/F 0) of PPD-CQDs responding to different concentrations of ethanol in Baijiu were compared to those of scE-Ws (20–80 vol%; Fig. 4B). Here, F 0 and F represent the emission intensity of PPD-CQDs in water and in different ethanol concentrations, respectively. Then, the same procedures were performed for other Light aroma Baijiu with ethanol contents of 42 and 65.8 vol%, respectively (Fig. S3). In Fig. 4B, as the ethanol concentration increases, F/F 0 shows an increasing trend over the whole testing range. Meanwhile, similar to the E-Ws model, there is a linear response of PPD-CQDs to real Baijiu at different ethanol contents. The PPD-CQDs probe shows a linear response for ethanol from 20 to 80 vol%, with a slope between 0.05 and 0.08 vol%, respectively. A large emission gap exists between scE-Ws and the selected Baijiu. The slopes of the selected Baijiu are obviously lower than those of the scE-Ws (0.13 vol%). Therefore, the fluorescent probe of PPD-CQDs cannot be directly used to detect the ethanol content in real Baijiu. The interference factors need to be found, and their correction should be performed accordingly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crucial factors leading to the emission gap of PPD-CQDs between Baijiu and scE-Ws
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence responses of PPD-CQDs to different amounts of lactic acid and furfural, as representative compounds in Baijiu, are shown in Fig. S4A and B, respectively. More than 50% of the emission of PPD-CQDs is quenched by 10 mM lactic acid, but less than 5% is quenched by furfural at a high concentration of 50 mM. Therefore, the interference of a small amount furfural can be negligible, but that of lactic acid cannot. Fig. S4C compares the interferences on PPD-CQDs emission by other often-involved compounds in Baijiu. This illustrates that except for lactic acid, which can quench the fluorescence intensity of PPD-CQDs, none of the compounds shows an obvious effect on PPD-CQDs emission. Therefore, lactic acid is crucial for quenching the fluorescence of PPD-CQDs in Baijiu. Accordingly, the effects of other possible organic acids in Baijiu need to be determined.

Organic acids are the most important substances in Baijiu, and their types and levels are closely related to the fermentation process and microbial type in Baijiu (Liu et al., 2020). In Baijiu, the total acid is composed mainly of lactic acid, malic acid, acetic acid, n-octanoic acid, propionic acid, caproic acid, heptanoic acid and citric acid. In the following experiment, their effects on the PPD-CQDs response were tested. The results in Fig. S4D indicate that all of them can quench the fluorescence intensity of PPD-CQDs. This suggests that the total contribution of these acids in Baijiu may be the source of the quenching response of PPD-CQDs.

Accordingly, the pH effect on the emission response of PPD-CQDs in ethanol was tested by changing the pH of the system (Fig. S5A), which showed a significant enhancing effect on PPD-CQDs emission along with a nonlinear increase in pH (Fig. S5B). This is due to the easier protonation of amine groups on the surface of PPD-CQDs in an acidic environment, which in turn leads to the aggregation of PPD-CQDs. At the same time, as the pH decreases, the protonation of amine (–NH3+) at the surface state will inhibit the complexing of PPD-CQDs and ethanol and consequently cause a slight blueshift and emission quenching (Chen et al., 2019), as illustrated in Fig. S5A.

In addition, Table S1 indicates that both E-Ws and Baijiu at different concentrations are acidic. However, the pH value of Baijiu is obviously lower than that of scE-Ws. Therefore, acidity is the direct cause of fluorescence quenching of PPD-CQDs in Baijiu. Thus, pH correction of Baijiu should be performed to eliminate its interference on ethanol determination in Baijiu.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correction of the emission deviation between scE-Ws and Baijiu through pH adjustment
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4C-F depict that upon NaOH titration, the fluorescence intensity of PPD-CQDs in each Baijiu increases gradually, finally approaching that in the scE-Ws. In particular, Fig. 4G shows that when the concentration of NaOH is higher than 10 mM, the fluorescence intensity of PPD-CQDs does not change with the addition of more NaOH. Such phenomena show that the fabricated PPD-CQDs can be used to detect the ethanol content of Baijiu after adding a fixed amount of NaOH to compensate for its relatively low pH value. Therefore, the following procedures will be performed to detect the ethanol content in Baijiu: after adding 10 μL NaOH (1.0 M) into 1 mL Baijiu, 10 μL of PPD-CQDs is titrated into the above solution, and the corresponding emission intensity at peak-point is measured immediately. Accordingly, the ethanol content in Baijiu can be calculated based on the working plot illustrated in Fig. 4H. Therefore, a fast and easily performed protocol for ethanol tests in Baijiu is established, which can be conveniently used for ethanol detection in the laboratory, in-market or for online product assay. The main advantages of this fluorescence method are fast testing with simple pretreatment, inexpensive equipment, and a simple detection system, which can directly display the fluorescence signal and ethanol concentrations. In particular, the red emission of PPD-CQDs will exclude the florescence interference coming from ingredients and the E–W cluster itself, guaranteeing the accuracy of ethanol determination in Baijiu. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ethanol contents in Baijiu.
ce:section.ce:para: For practical sensing applications, ethanol quantifications were carried out for real Baijiu samples using PPD-CQDs as fluorescence probes after pH correction simply by adding 10 μL NaOH (1.0 M) into 1 mL Baijiu. Scheme 1 depicts the processes for in-lab and online ethanol quantification. First, we used nine types of Light aroma Baijiu purchased from a local supermarket with ethanol contents of 39.9, 42, 52, 53, 56, and 65.8 vol% in China as representatives to evaluate the ethanol content by the fluorescence probe of PPD-CQDs. Table 1 summarizes the results calculated from working plots of F/F0 = 0.13⋅Cethanol-0.36. The accuracy rate is located between 95.0% and 100.7%. The RSDs were calculated based on the results of five replicates, which are less than 1.5% and indicate that the designed method can be used to detect the ethanol content in commercial Baijiu with light aroma.

Among Chinese Baijiu, the Light aroma is relatively light in taste and contains fewer additives than the strong and sauce aromas (Wei et al., 2020). Thus, ethanol detection is relatively simpler and easier than in other Baijiu. To assay the biological replicates of the developed method, we also tested Baijiu samples with strong and sauce aromas, and other aromas. Table 1 shows that the recovery rates of strong, sauce, and other aromas of Baijiu were between 93.0 and 102.8%, and the corresponding RSDs (n = 5) were between 0.4 and 1.7%, indicating that the developed method can be used to determine ethanol contents in various aroma types of Baijiu. As illustrated for the Strong (Fig. S6A) and Sauce-strong (Fig. S6B) aromas as representatives, the results are perfectly consistent with the indicated ethanol degrees. Particularly, as illustrated in Fig. S6C, the strong emission from Sauce aroma Baijiu can be excluded perfectly by the red-emitted PPD-CQDs, which are almost located below 550 nm in the emission spectra. Meanwhile, we also tested the initial pH and that after correction for each Baijiu sample. As shown in Table 1, after the pH correction via the addition of 10 μL of 1.0 M NaOH, the pH values for all test Baijiu samples were essentially located between 5.0 and 6.2. Although each depends on the initial pH of Baijiu strongly, the close approaching of them to that of E-Ws indeed verify the great contribution of pH correction on the accurate ethanol determination in Baijiu.

Unfortunately, the evaluated ethanol contents in another Sauce aroma Baijiu (Maotai Yingbin and Guizhou Yingbin) are relatively lower than the indications (Table 1), which may be related to the higher contents of furfural and acetal in the Sauce aroma Baijiu (Li et al., 2021). In fact, as shown in Fig. S4C, the testing of furfural and acetal on PPD-CQDs emission indeed shows some quenching. Therefore, a further strategy to eliminate the effects of aldehyde compounds on ethanol detection in Sauce aroma Baijiu is necessary, which needs to be further explored in the future. Finally, the Baijiu samples, casually collected from online products of distillation (as it covers widely from high to low ethanol contents), were tested by using PPD-CQDs. Of note, except for pH correction, no other pretreatment was performed on the samples. The evaluated ethanol contents are listed in Table S2, which were verified by using HPLC in parallel. The high recovery rates (98.8%-101.8%) and low RSDs less than 1.7% confirm the robustness of the fluorescent PPD-CQDs in online ethanol content detection after a simple pH correction.

In summary, the developed and validated method, based on red-emitted PPD-CQDs and a simple pH correction, can determine the ethanol content in Chinese Baijiu quickly and accurately. Initially, the PPD-CQDs can determine ethanol in E-Ws based on the linear response to ethanol in a wide range of 20–80 vol%. However, the deviated results were obtained in actual Baijiu because of the relatively lower pH than the E-Ws, even at the same ethanol concentrations. However, such deviation can return to the right position after a simple pH correction using NaOH solution. Therefore, PPD-CQDs can accurately determine ethanol in actual Baijiu. In particular, the red emission of PPD-CQDs eliminated the interference of intrinsic fluorescence in Baijiu, which often appears in the spectral range below 550 nm. In addition, the online determination of ethanol by PPD-CQDs showed high agreement with the HPLC data, demonstrating its accurate determination of ethanol in Chinese Baijiu and great potential for other alcoholic beverages worldwide.

Xin-He Duan: Data curation, Investigation, Software, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yi Li: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Hong-Wei Li: Data curation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yuqing Wu: Data curation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their gratitude to the financial support of NSFC (Nos. 21875085), Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Plan Project (No. 20230204040YY), and the Innovation & Open Program of the State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.136733.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
